title: Reduction and collectors

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Reduction and collectors; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Lambda Expressions and Stream API learning paths; atomic topic: Reduction and collectors
sources.claim: Reduction combines values with an identity and associative accumulator, while collectors manage mutable result containers through supplier, accumulator, combiner, and finisher contracts.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Reduction combines values with an identity and associative accumulator, while collectors manage mutable result containers through supplier, accumulator, combiner, and finisher contracts.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Integer values reduce with identity 0 and Integer::sum, producing 0 for an empty stream and the same result under valid regrouping. Your goal is to choose and implement a reduction or collector with correct identity and combination behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration, mutation and immutability; Interfaces; Stream pipelines. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Reduction combines values with an identity and associative accumulator, while collectors manage mutable result containers through supplier, accumulator, combiner, and finisher contracts. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A non-associative operation can change under regrouping, an incorrect identity corrupts empty or parallel results, duplicate map keys need a merge policy, and mutable reduction must not share unsafe containers. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Any binary operation works in reduce because streams always combine values from left to right exactly once.

includes: Aggregate values using associative reductions and standard collectors.
Choose and implement a reduction or collector with correct identity and combination behavior.
excludes: A non-associative operation can change under regrouping, an incorrect identity corrupts empty or parallel results, duplicate map keys need a merge policy, and mutable reduction must not share unsafe containers.

outcomes: Choose and implement a reduction or collector with correct identity and combination behavior.

examples.title: Reduction and collectors: direct language case
examples.situation: Integer values reduce with identity 0 and Integer::sum, producing 0 for an empty stream and the same result under valid regrouping.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Integer values reduce with identity 0 and Integer::sum, producing 0 for an empty stream and the same result under valid regrouping.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use an associative operation with a true identity for reduction, and choose a collector whose combination and duplicate-key behavior matches the result contract.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use an associative operation with a true identity for reduction, and choose a collector whose combination and duplicate-key behavior matches the result contract.

examples.title: Reduction and collectors: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: toMap receives an explicit merge function when two records can share a key, instead of failing unpredictably on production data.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: toMap receives an explicit merge function when two records can share a key, instead of failing unpredictably on production data.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A non-associative operation can change under regrouping, an incorrect identity corrupts empty or parallel results, duplicate map keys need a merge policy, and mutable reduction must not share unsafe containers.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Any binary operation works in reduce because streams always combine values from left to right exactly once.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and implement a reduction or collector with correct identity and combination behavior.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any binary operation works in reduce because streams always combine values from left to right exactly once.
misconceptions.correction: Use an associative operation with a true identity for reduction, and choose a collector whose combination and duplicate-key behavior matches the result contract.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Any binary operation works in reduce because streams always combine values from left to right exactly once.

analogy.example: a one-pass processing line that transforms, filters, combines, or explicitly reports a missing result can introduce Reduction and collectors, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: lambdas, stream stages, reduction, and Optional
limitations: laziness, target typing, associativity, parallel regrouping, and single-use traversal have no complete conveyor analogy
